bids.reasoning: The opponent's starting offer provides me with the lowest possible utility across all issues, contradicting their emotional appeal for an agreement. Since it is the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0), I will ignore the cheap talk, hold firmly to my own preferences, and open with a bid that maximizes my utility.

It is extremely early in the negotiation (t=0.02). The opponent has repeated their initial offer which is minimal utility for me, despite their emotional appeals for a partnership. I will make a very minor concession on my lowest-weighted issue (issueD) to signal flexibility while keeping my most important issues at their maximum value.

The time fraction is very early (0.04) and the opponent has repeated their initial lowball offer of valueD for all issues, paired with emotional appeals. I must treat their messages as standard negotiation tactics and rely on actual bid movement. I will make a minor concession on issueC and issueD, which are my least important issues, to test if they are willing to reciprocate.

Since the negotiation is still very early at t=0.06, there is no need to make large concessions, especially on high-priority issues. The opponent is employing emotional appeals ("desperate for this specific arrangement") and has made a slight concession on issueA. Because issueA is my most heavily weighted issue (0.4), I will capitalize on their willingness to move there by holding my ground on it. Instead, I will offer a small concession on my least important issue, issueD, moving its value to valueC. This reduces my utility slightly to ~0.89 while showing willingness to compromise.

The negotiation is still very early (t=0.08). The opponent has sent several emotion-laden messages trying to draw out large concessions without substantially moving their own offers (only a minor concession on issueA). I will ignore the opponent's emotional appeals as unbinding cheap talk, but I will make a strategic concession by fully yielding on my least important issue (issueD), hoping to trade it for agreement on the issues most critical to my utility (issueA and issueB).

The opponent's messages employ an emotional appeal tactic ("practically begging", "survival"), which I am treating as cheap talk since their actual bidding behavior shows extreme stubbornness, holding firm on almost all their preferred values. Since we are still in the early phase of the negotiation (t=0.1), I will maintain high utility by holding firm on my most valuable issues (issueA and issueB) while cautiously conceding on issueC to signal a willingness to continue negotiating.

The opponent is relying on repeated emotional appeals to push for concessions, but their bidding history reveals a willingness to concede on issueA (moving from valueD to valueC to valueB). Since my weight for issueA is the highest, I will hold firm on valueA. To keep negotiations moving forward cooperatively at this early stage, I will concede slightly on issueB and issueC while allowing them to keep valueD on issueD, targeting a utility of around 0.78.

The opponent continues to rely on emotional appeals in their messages, which I am treating as cheap talk, yet their offers show a very slow concession pattern solely on issueA. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.14), there is no need to make drastic concessions. I will maintain issueA and issueB near my top utilities to maximize my score and offer a small concession by shifting issueC and issueD combinations to explore their true preferences.

The opponent is heavily employing an emotional appeal strategy while maintaining low-utility offers. They previously conceded slightly on issueA before retracting it to concede slightly on issueB, and they have not moved on issueC or issueD at all. Since we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.16), I will hold firm on my most important issues (issueA and issueB) while fully conceding on issueC (my second-lowest priority, which they seem to value highly since they haven't budged from valueD).

The opponent relies entirely on emotional appeals ("practically begging", "entire livelihood"), which is cheap talk that I will ignore in favor of reading their actual numeric bids. Their recent offers have alternated concessions on issue A and issue B before pulling back on A, suggesting they are exploring combinations but still anchoring low. Because it is still early (t=0.18), I will protect my highest-weight issue (A) by keeping it at valueA, while offering concessions on my lower-weight issues (C and D) and presenting valueB for issue B, targeting a solid mid-0.70s utility overall.

The opponent has made a significant concession on my most critical issue (issueA: valueA) while holding firm on their values for the other issues. Their emotional appeals continue to act as a negotiation tactic, but their bidding action provides a clear signal that they are willing to concede issueA. Since it is still early in the negotiation (t=0.2), I will lock in issueA at valueA and issueB at valueA, but I will make a genuine concession by offering valueD on my least important issue (issueD) and valueC on issueC, targeting a strong utility of ~0.81. This acknowledges their persistent bids for valueD on the later issues while protecting my high-weight utilities.

The opponent is relying heavily on emotional appeals ("desperately need", "begging") while keeping most of their offers anchored at their maximum utility ('valueD'). Despite this cheap talk, they previously conceded to 'valueA' on issueA and recently to 'valueC' on issueC. Because I am still early in the negotiation (t=0.22), I will hold firm on issueA where my weight is highest, concede slightly on issueC to match their recent offer, and request 'valueB' on issueB, maintaining a high utility for myself.

The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals and fabricated desperation, which I treat as non-binding cheap talk. However, their bids show steady concessions, particularly offering 'valueA' on my highest-priority issue (issueA) and 'valueC' on issueC. I will hold firm on issueA to maximize my utility, whilst offering 'valueB' for issues B and C, and gradually conceding to 'valueC' on my lowest-priority issue (issueD) to maintain a strong utility of approximately 0.81 while leaving room for future compromise.

The opponent's emotional appeals are persistent but lack specific issue constraints, so I will treat them as a continuous tactical attempt while focusing entirely on their bidding behavior. They have shown a willingness to concede significantly on issueA (offering valueA at t=0.20) and issueB (up to valueB), while aggressively holding issueC and issueD at valueD. Since issueA and issueB are my most valuable issues, I will propose to secure valueA for both, while fully conceding to their apparent preferred valueD for my low-weight issues (issueC and issueD). This yields a strong utility of 0.76 for myself while giving them their ideal choices on two of the four issues.

The negotiation is still in the early phase (t=0.28). The opponent's messages rely heavily on emotional appeals with very little concrete structural information, although their varied offers across different issues suggest they are searching for tradeoffs. I am targeting a high utility close to 0.87 by securing my high-weight issues (A and B) at top value, while giving slight concessions on my less important issues (C and D).

The opponent is relying heavily on emotional appeals in their messages, which I am treating as cheap talk. They have shown some willingness to give me `valueA` on `issueA` (at t=0.20) and `valueB` on `issueB` (at t=0.26), but they strongly insist on `valueD` for most issues. I will target a utility of ~0.85 by insisting on high values for my most critical issues (`issueA` and `issueB`) while conceding on `issueD`, which carries my lowest weight.

The current time fraction is 0.32, meaning there is still plenty of time, so I will prioritize keeping my utility relatively high (above 0.84). The opponent frequently uses emotional appeals, but I will treat these statements as cheap talk and rely entirely on their actual bids. They have shown some flexibility on issues A and B, so I will hold firm on valueA for my highest-weight issues (issueA and issueB), while conceding slightly on my lower-weight issues (issueC and issueD) to valueC to see if it brings us closer to an agreement.

The opponent continues to attach highly emotional messages ("desperately need this", "survival", "pleading"), but these are treated as unverified cheap talk because their actual offers remain highly favorable to themselves. Their recent offer at t=0.34 concessions slightly on issues B and C, so I will reciprocate modestly. I will maintain valueA for my highest priority issue (issueA) and issueC, while conceding on issueB to valueB and fully yielding on issueD to valueD, as issueD carries my lowest preference weight and clearly matters significantly to them.

The opponent is consistently making emotional appeals, which I am disregarding as cheap talk. However, they are making incremental concessions across various issues. To maintain my utility above 0.80 while presenting a new combination, I will insist on my highest weights (issueA and issueB at valueA) while conceding heavily on my lower-weighted issueC (offering valueD, their preferred value) and holding issueD at valueA to balance the utility target.

At t=0.38, we are approaching the mid-game. The opponent's messages consist of repetitive emotional appeals about their "livelihood" being in danger, which I am disregarding as cheap talk tactics. However, looking at their actual bidding behavior, they have recently offered `valueB` for `issueC`, and previously offered `valueB` for `issueB`. I will capitalize on these signals by integrating those values into my bid, thus finding a compromise, while maintaining my requirement for `valueA` on my most important matter, `issueA`.

The opponent's messages consist of repetitive emotional appeals claiming they are stretching their limits, which I dismiss as cheap talk, especially since their actual bids remain heavily skewed toward valueD across the board. Since we are at t=0.4, I will maintain strong positions on my most heavily weighted issues (issueA and issueB) while showing flexibility on my lower-weighted issues (issueC and issueD) to constructively explore a compromise.

I am targeting a utility of roughly 0.78 since we are approaching the mid-game (t=0.42). The opponent's messages are heavy with emotional appeals and fabricated desperation, which I treat as cheap talk, analyzing their actual bids instead. Their history demonstrates an inflexible stance on issueD (valueD) but shows flexibility on issueA and issueC over time. Therefore, I will completely concede issueD, hold firm on my highest priority issueA, and offer middle-ground values for issueB and issueC.

The opponent is consistently using emotional appeals, which I will treat as cheap talk. They have shown some flexibility on issues A, B, and C over time (sometimes offering valueB for them), but their recent offer slightly deviates. Given we are at time fraction 0.44, I will maintain high utility on my most important issue (A), but concede slightly on C and D to find a mutually acceptable combination while keeping my overall utility above 0.75.

The current time fraction is 0.46. Although the opponent's messages are purely emotional appeals without much concrete strategic substance, their bidding pattern clearly reveals that their lowest-weighted issues are issueA and issueB, as they conceded those earlier, while holding firm on issueD and issueC. Since issueA and issueB are my highest-priority issues (weights 0.4 and 0.3), this presents an excellent integrative opportunity. I will hold firm on my top two priorities and make calculated concessions on issueC and issueD to reflect their preferences, aiming for a mutually acceptable tradeoff.

The opponent is aggressively employing emotional appeals claiming desperation, but I will ignore these empty signals and focus on the data. Their latest offer includes `issueB: valueA`, which aligns with my top preference for my second most important issue, while they clearly favor `valueD` for `issueC` and `issueD`. At roughly the halfway mark, I will accept their concession on `issueB`, maintain `valueA` on `issueA` to preserve my utility around 0.81, and offer `valueD` on `issueC` and `valueB` on `issueD` as realistic trade-offs.

The opponent continues to make regular emotional appeals and has indicated a strong preference for `valueD` for most issues, particularly `issueC` and `issueD`. While they recently offered `issueB: valueA` and `issueA: valueB`, I want to maintain `valueA` on my highest weight issue (`issueA`) while showing flexibility elsewhere. I am proposing a staggered concession (`valueA`, `valueB`, `valueC`, `valueD`), delivering a solid utility of 0.733 to myself while matching their likely priorities on the smaller issues.

The opponent's messages consist entirely of emotional appeals which I will treat as cheap talk. They recently made a concession on issueA (valueA) but pulled back on issueB (valueD), while simultaneously favoring valueD on issueD across almost all offers. Exploring different combinations to find Pareto-optimal tradeoffs, I will propose a bid focusing on keeping issueA at valueA while finding a middle ground on issues B, C, and providing valueC on issueD, aiming for a utility around 0.81 to leave room for further concessions later in the negotiation.

The opponent is continuously sending emotional appeals claiming desperation, but their offers are bouncing around and occasionally conceding strong values for my top priorities, such as A=valueA or B=valueA. At t=0.54, we are just past the midpoint, so there's no need to aggressively drop my standing. I will lock in my top priority issue (issueA) and slightly compromise on lower-priority issues (issueC and issueD) to find combinations the opponent has signaled they might tolerate, aiming for a utility of around 0.78.

The opponent's messages consist of recurring emotional appeals devoid of specific constraints, so I will treat them as standard non-binding negotiation tactics. In their most recent bid, they proposed 'valueB' for issues B and C, showing possible alignment on those terms. I am proposing an offer that meets them on 'valueB' for issues B, C, and D while holding firm on my most critical issue (issue A) at 'valueA', securing a strong utility around 0.84 as we pass the midpoint of our negotiation time.

The time fraction is 0.58, so we are entering the mid-to-late phase. The opponent's messages are exclusively emotional appeals and begging (fake/fabricated distress), which I will disregard. Their bidding history shows they strongly favor valueD for most issues but have recently proposed C for issues C and D, and previously conceded on B to valueB. I will propose an offer that holds firm on my most important issue (A), retains high utility on B, but concedes to the opponent's demonstrated preferences for C and D to find potential common ground.
